coredata.dc:title: One step production of Se doped carbon dots for rapid sensing of tetracycline in real water sample
coredata.dc:creator: Chauhan, Pooja
Chaudhary, Savita
coredata.dc:description: In the present decade, the utilization of antibiotics at augmented rate has caused harmful environmental pollution. The resistance rate of bacteria against antibiotics has become faster due to excessive employment of antibiotics. Therefore, it is mandatory to develop a new, sustainable and environment friendly nanohybrid system which can easily detect antibiotic especially tetracycline. It is prevalently found drug pollutant which can easily contaminate food, aquatic species and human health. While keeping all the facts during consideration, a simple and one step scheme is followed for the development of selenium supported carbon dots (Se@CDs) nano hybrids by utilizing commonly available natural sugars such as sucrose, fructose and glucose as a reducing and stabilizing agent. The CDs were prepared from the waste coconut husk. The developed nano-hybrid exhibits excellent optical and emission properties which suggest their further applications. Additionally, different analytical studies have been carried out to evaluate their physicochemical properties. Due to their outstanding analytical behavior, they have been successfully utilized in the sensitive and specific detection of tetracycline (TC) in presence of different interfering ions. The glucose derived Se@CDs shows maximum quenching effect towards TC. The value of detection limit and binding constant were found to be 155 nM and 517 nM−1. Further, their recovery studies have been carried out in different real samples. The obtained findings suggest that the developed Se@CDs can be successfully explored in biological fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Selenium
Tetracycline
Selectivity
Glucose
Fructose and sucrose

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Methodology for the synthesis of nano hybrid system
Application of developed Se@CDs in tetracycline sensing

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Council of Scientific and Industrial Research, India

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Haryana State Council for Science and Technology
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Haryana State Council for Science and Technology

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Pooja Chauhan is thankful to the CSIR open SRF funding 2019. Savita Chaudhary is thankful to DST Inspire Faculty award [IFACH- 17], Haryana State Council for Science and Technology [HSCSIT/R&D/2020/476], DST Chandigarh and DST Purse grants II for financial assistance. Authors are thankful to Panjab University SAIF CIL and Department of Environment Studies Panjab University Chandigarh for technical support.

Pooja Chauhan is thankful to the CSIR open SRF funding 2019. Savita Chaudhary is thankful to DST Inspire Faculty award [IFACH- 17], Haryana State Council for Science and Technology [HSCSIT/R&D/2020/476], DST Chandigarh and DST Purse grants II for financial assistance. Authors are thankful to Panjab University SAIF CIL and Department of Environment Studies Panjab University Chandigarh for technical support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a–c) Uv–visible absorption spectrum (inset shows the wavelength maxima with corresponding concentration) and (d–f) fluorescence spectrum (λex. = 290 nm and λem. = 430 nm) of prepared S–Se@CDs, F–Se@CDs and G-Se@CDs.
(a) FTIR and (b) XRD spectrum of developed S–Se@CDs, F–Se@CDs and G-Se@CDs.
Representation of (a–c) TGA and (d–f) DSC spectrum of prepared S–Se@CDs, F–Se@CDs and G-Se@CDs.
(a–c) HRTEM images (inset showing respective SAED pattern) and (d–f) FESEM images of prepared S–Se@CDs, F–Se@CDs and G-Se@CDs.

(a) Fluorescence spectrum showing concentration variation of tetracycline (1000-10 μM), (b) interference study and (c) pH study of tetracycline in presence of developed G-Se@CDs system (λex. = 290 nm and λem. = 430 nm).
(a) Stern-Volmer plot and (b) B–H plot of developed G-Se@CDs in presence of different concentration of tetracycline.
Recovery study of developed (G-Se@CDs with tetracycline) system (a) in presence of different water mediums and (b) in different sources (λex. = 290 nm and λem. = 430 nm).
Schematic illustration showing the sensing mechanism of tetracycline (TC) by G-Se@CDs.

head.ce:title: One step production of Se doped carbon dots for rapid sensing of tetracycline in real water sample

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Centre of Advanced Studies in Chemistry, Panjab University, Chandigarh, 160014, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Centre of Advanced Studies in Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Centre of Advanced Studies in Chemistry, Panjab University, Chandigarh, 160014, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present decade, the utilization of antibiotics at augmented rate has caused harmful environmental pollution. The resistance rate of bacteria against antibiotics has become faster due to excessive employment of antibiotics. Therefore, it is mandatory to develop a new, sustainable and environment friendly nanohybrid system which can easily detect antibiotic especially tetracycline. It is prevalently found drug pollutant which can easily contaminate food, aquatic species and human health. While keeping all the facts during consideration, a simple and one step scheme is followed for the development of selenium supported carbon dots (Se@CDs) nano hybrids by utilizing commonly available natural sugars such as sucrose, fructose and glucose as a reducing and stabilizing agent. The CDs were prepared from the waste coconut husk. The developed nano-hybrid exhibits excellent optical and emission properties which suggest their further applications. Additionally, different analytical studies have been carried out to evaluate their physicochemical properties. Due to their outstanding analytical behavior, they have been successfully utilized in the sensitive and specific detection of tetracycline (TC) in presence of different interfering ions. The glucose derived Se@CDs shows maximum quenching effect towards TC. The value of detection limit and binding constant were found to be 155 nM and 517 nM−1. Further, their recovery studies have been carried out in different real samples. The obtained findings suggest that the developed Se@CDs can be successfully explored in biological fields.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to rapid utilization of fossil fuels and augmented environment pollution, the surrounding atmosphere urgently requires a new, sustainable and environmentally friendly strategic material [1–3]. In literature, Carbon has been considered as the most abundant and non-toxic element in the earth crust. Carbon containing carbon dots (CDs) have various advantages such as low cost, easy to manufacture, biocompatible nature and wide application range. However, they have some short comings such as low water solubility, electrical conductivity, wide band gap and small surface area which confines its utilization in application field [4–8]. To overcome such short comings, Selenium (Se) can act as an electron withdrawing group and can enhance the interparticle distance between CDs. The field of selenium is flourishing day by day in present decade [9,10]. It is considered as the chalcogen element and owns various physical, chemical and biological properties which open its wide range application scope. It is a metalloid which possesses both metal and non-metal properties. The strong covalent bond formation tendency of Se with carbon material makes it a strong and stable material [11,12]. As compared to other metal and non-metals, Se hold various extra advantages such as cheap cost, easy metabolization in the body and relatively safe for the environment. In literature, various reports have been published which suggest the additional benefits of Se with CDs. For example, Yang et al. (2021) prepared selenium doped nitrogen rich carbon nanosheets through oxidation selenization process. The developed nanosheets acts as an anode material for enhanced storage of Na-ion in batteries [13]. Further, Cao et al. (2020) synthesized selenium doped carbon dots from the glucose by utilizing simple and green methodology [14]. The developed system exhibits outstanding antibacterial activity against Xanthomonas campestris which causes harmful cabbage black rot disease and results in high economic loss.

The loading of Se into CDs surface might be considered as an efficient method with extraordinary physicochemical properties. The Se supported CDs might serve as a recyclable heterogenous material with wide field applications. To augment the efficacy of CDs and resolve their drawbacks, many experimentations were carried out in literature [15–17]. While keeping all the facts in mind, Se has been induced as a heteroatom during the synthesis of CDs [18]. Therefore, in this paper we have paid full attention in the development of selenium supported carbon dots (Se@CDs). The prepared materials possess wide range of applications such as catalysis, food chemistry, agriculture, industrial field, environmental protection and so on. Here, we wish to summarize the application part of developed in biological component sensing.

Further, the field of antibiotics emerging in a golden manner especially in husbandry additive, drug delivery and sterilization. Out of various antibiotics, Tetracycline (TC) has been considered as the widely utilized and broad-spectrum antibiotic for disease related to bacterial infections [19,20]. It consists of a tetracyclic nucleus through which different functional groups are attached. But the excessive utilization of antibiotics causing serious consequences on human health and environmental species [21–23]. In literature various conventional methods (high performance liquid chromatography, mass spectroscopy, thin layer chromatography and enzyme immunoassay) have been reported for the sensing of antibiotics but they have various drawbacks (low detection limit, less accurate, non-rapid, costly, laborious and time consuming) [24]. Hence it is mandatory and highly significant to develop such rapid, simple and cost-effective system which can easily detect TC such as fluorescence spectroscopy. So, in this paper, Se@CDs has been synthesized by utilizing various sugars as a reducing agent. According to the best of our knowledge this is first report which utilized both Se and CDs together to form nano hybrid system. The developed nanohybrid system reflects excellent physicochemical properties and enhanced morphological alterations. They have been further utilized in field of biological component sensing such as tetracycline through fluorescence emission spectroscopy. The fluorescence studies reveal the excellent selectivity and specificity of developed Se@CDs towards TC with good detection limit value. The recovery studies have been carried out using various real water samples and sources. The developed sensing strategy is considered as the highly efficient methodology for the detection of tetracycline in presence of other interfering ions.

Selenous acid (H2SeO3) was used as a precursor and procured from Himedia with 98% purity. Sucrose and d-fructose was used as a reducing and capping agent and purchased from Himedia with >99% purity. D-(+) glucose was acquired from Sigma Aldrich having >99.5% purity. Tetracycline hydrochloride, oxytetracycline (OxTc) and doxycycline (Dxc) was obtained from TCI chemicals (>98% purity). Different amino acids (cysteine, arginine, serine, tryptophan, histidine, glutamic acid (GA) and aspartic acid (AA)) were procured were Sigma Aldrich with 99% purity. Different metal salts of chloride anions (Ba2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Na+, Pb2+, Fe2+ and Fe3+) were obtained from Avra chemical synthesis with 98% purity. Coconut coir waste was purchased from the local market of sector-15, Chandigarh. Deionized water was used as a solvent for the preparation of all the samples. All the chemicals are used as such without doing any further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Methodology for the synthesis of nano hybrid system

ce:list-item.ce:label: (a)
ce:list-item.ce:para: Preparation of coconut waste derived carbon dots

ce:section.ce:para:
The successful synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) has been carried out using waste coconut husk as a predecessor. The facile, one step thermal calcination treatment was performed due to involvement of multiple reactions (decarboxylation and polymerization). For the synthesis, waste coconut husk was purchased from the local market and cut into tiny pieces using sterilized scissor. The obtained pieces were grinded well using electrical pastel mortar to obtain fine powder. The powder (5 g) was taken in sterilized silica crucible and then placed in muffle furnace at 350 °C temperature for 2 h. After 2 h of continuous heating, silica crucible has been removed out and allowed to cool till room temperature. 1 g of obtained brown powder was dispersed in 500 ml deionized water and allowed to stir at room temperature for 15 h. The prepared suspension was allowed to centrifuge at 4000 rpm for 5 min to remove aggregated particles. The obtained supernatant was collected and kept at 2 °C for the further analysis. The small amount of supernatant was heated at 100 °C for obtaining powder form of CDs [8,25].
(b)
Preparation of in-situ selenium supported carbon dots (Se@CDs) or nano hybrid system

The synthesis of developed (Se@CDs) nanocatalyst has been carried out using simple green and environmentally friendly approach. For the systematic and novel hybrid system preparation, CDs (1, 2, 3, 4 and 5 mg) has been mixed with selenous acid at different concentration intervals (10, 20, 30, 40 and 50 mM). The natural reducing sugars such as fructose, glucose and sucrose have been utilized for the complete reduction of selenous acid (IV) into selenium (0) which results in the formation of fructose reduced Se supported CDs (F–Se@CDs), glucose reduced Se supported CDs (G-Se@CDs) and sucrose reduced Se supported CDs (S–Se@CDs). The yielded mixture was heated at 90 °C for 15 min and it was observed that light grey solution changes into light reddish-orange color which suggest the complete formation of Se@CDs hybrid system [9,26].

The absorption profile and optical behavior of developed nanohybrids was analyzed by using JASCO V-750 UV–visible spectrophotometer having quartz cuvette with a fixed path length of 1.0 cm. The baseline correction was evaluated out before carrying out sample analysis in the presence of preferred solvent. The emission profile of was investigated by using Perkin Elmer LS55 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The excitation and emission slit width of fluorescence spectrophotometer was kept fixed at 10 nm with a scan speed of 500 nm/min. The spectrum profile of respective samples was recorded after subtracting the background correction. The IR spectrum of prepared nanoparticles was obtained using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (Perkin Elmer Spectrum 400) under working range of 400–4000 cm−1 having average scanning rate of 100 with KBr pellets and spectral resolution of 2 cm−1. During the respective analysis, powder form of prepared NPs was utilized. The thermal stability and heat capacity of powdered Se@CDs were evaluated by using thermo-gravimetric analysis (TGA-DSC) under the atmosphere of nitrogen with a temperature range of 0–1000 °C in an aluminium crucible having a fixed interval of 5 °C/min (METTLER TOLEDO (Q20)). The nanocrystalline structural analysis and size range of developed particles was evaluated out by using X-ray powder diffractometer (Panalytical X'Pert Pro) with monochromatic CuKα radiation at 40 KV having scanning range of 2θ = 10–80° with a fixed scan rate of 2 deg/min. Hitachi (JEOLJSM 7500F) field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) with energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis was used to investigate the purity, morphological screening and elemental mapping of developed nanohybrids. The power Sonic 405 sonicator was utilized to develop the standardized and consistent solution of Se@CDs and tetracycline. Remi R-24 centrifuge was utilized for the centrifugation purpose of developed CDs. The size distribution profile of developed particles along with their topographical changes was explored through high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) on JEOL 2100F instrument at 200 Kv. The zeta potential analysis (Anton Par) was carried out to evaluate the charge present on the surface of Se@CDs where water was used as a dispersant.

The developed Se doped CDs suggests their complete formation as seen by naked eyes. The different characterization techniques were investigated on Se@CDs to evaluate their physicochemical properties. In order to evaluate the absorption behavior of developed Se@CDs, UV–visible spectroscopy was utilized in range 200–800 nm. From the absorption spectrum, it was observed that mainly two characteristic absorption band was raised at 280 and 320 nm (Fig. 1 (a-c)). The absorption peak at 280 nm refers to π-π* transition and 320 nm corresponds to the n-π* transition which is mainly associated with surface states such as CO, C–N and C–Se of Se@CDs [7,8]. The slight variation in the absorption behavior has been observed which was mainly associated with the utilization of different reducing and capping agent such as sucrose, fructose and glucose. From the obtained results, it can be concluded that on increasing the concentration of Se (10, 20, 30, 40 and 50 mM) in Se@CDs nano hybrid, the linear enhancement in the absorption behavior was observed. The insignificant discrepancy in the position of peak was ascribed to the augmentation in the refractive index values of neighboring atmosphere. Further, the emission behaviour of developed nano hybrid was also studied. It was found that on augmenting the concentration of Se from 10 mM to 50 mM, the emission intensity does not increase significantly due to the slight non-fluorescent behavior of Se (Fig. 1(d-f)). Additionally, the effect of CDs was also studied on the Se surface and it was observed that on increasing the concentration of CDs from 2 mg to 12 mg, the absorption emission intensity increases linearly with great significance (Fig. S1). Further, the absorption data of developed Se@CDs nano hybrid at different concentration of Se and CDs, has been employed to evaluate the value of band gap between valence band and conduction band by plotting a graph between (ϵhν)2 vs hν as a function of different concentration of CDs and Se source as mentioned above [11]. The Tauc plots were drawn at different concentration of Se and CDs in the present Se@CDs nano hybrid system (Fig. S2). The obtained band gap values show higher value as compared to the bulk material which suggest the creation of smaller size NPs (Table S1). The obtained value of band gap has been further utilized to calculate the value of radius, agglomeration number, valence band and conduction band energy [27]. The brief description about the respective parameters have been represented in the supporting file (Table S2).

Further, the pH study of the prepared Se@CDs has been carried out in range 2–12 by using UV–visible absorption and fluorescence emission spectroscopy (Fig. S3). From the absorption spectrum, it was inferred that, on increasing the pH of system from 2 to 12 the value of optical density increases and slight bathochromic shift (blue shift) was observed whereas opposite behaviour was observed in fluorescence study. In emission spectrum, it was observed that on increasing the pH of the reaction media, the emission intensity slightly decreases due to slight instability of the developed nano hybrid in basic medium. Simultaneously, protonation and deprotonation of present functional group also takes place. The presence of excess of hydroxyl groups on the surface decreases the emission profile. From the obtained data, it can be inferred that the acidic medium is most stable media for the developed Se@CDs nano hybrid system (Fig. S3) as compared to the basic media. But the developed system could efficiently work in the basic medium also due to the formation of stable structure. Additionally, the Se@CDs can be more stabilized in basic medium by adding some kind of polymer or amine functionalized group. Further, the presence of different functional and complete formation of Se@CDs was investigated out through FTIR spectroscopy. The similar kind of peaks observed in F–Se@CDs, G-Se@CDs and S–Se@CDs due to the presence of same kind of functional group in their periphery (Fig. 2 (a)). The broad peak at 3366 cm−1 corresponds to the absorption band of hydroxyl (O–H) group. The small peaks at 2934 and 1651 cm−1 were associated with the stretching vibration of C–H and CO group. The sharp N–H deformation band corresponds to the 1452 cm−1. The small peaks at 1122 and 1022 cm−1 refers to the CC and C–O stretching vibrations. The band at 617 cm−1 associated with stretching and bending vibrations of C–H and = C–H group and suggests the presence of alkyl and aryl group. From here, it can be concluded that, the presence of all the functional group suggests the formation of complete Se@CDs.

The orientation of crystal structure of developed Se@CDs was investigated out through X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) analysis (Fig. 2(b)). The major peaks obtained at 2θ = 28.43°, 40.60°, 50.31°, 58.60°, 66.57° and 74.01° which corresponds to the (002), (110), (200), (112), (220) and (113) miller indices. The size of developed Se@CDs was calculated through Debye Scherrer formula and found out to be 28.5, 28.3 and 27.5 nm for S–Se@CDs, F–Se@CDs and G-Se@CDs utilizing the diffraction peak at 28.4° (002 plane). The value of dislocation density (δ = 1/D2) has been calculated out from the average size value and shown in Table S3. The diffraction peaks were found to be sharper due to the presence of SeNPs. It is notable to observe that XRD spectrum of developed Se@CDs using three reducing agents (glucose, fructose and sucrose) have been found almost identical irrespective of the saccharide. The diffraction peaks observed in case of glucose saccharide were found to be smoother as compared to the fructose and sucrose as reducing agent. Further, the presence of CDs has stabilized the formation of SeNPs and prevents their agglomeration tendency. From here it can be concluded that the prepared carbon dots have been developed in nano range. The presence of some noise peaks might be due to the presence of saccharides. Further, XRD data have been applied to estimate the different parameters such as strain, stress and energy density values by applying uniform deformation model (UDM), uniform stress deformation model (USDM) and uniform deformation energy density model (UDEDM). Different graphs were plotted for the respected models of the developed Se@CDs (Fig. S4). The respective values have been represented in Table S4 and their brief description has been represented in supporting file.

Further, to evaluate the thermal behaviour of developed Se@CDs nano hybrid, TGA analysis has been carried out in range 0–1000 °C (Fig. 3 (a-c)). From the obtained thermogram, it was found that two major weight losses observed. In case of S–Se@CDs, first weight loss (24%) was observed at 250 °C and second weight loss (38%) was observed at 990 °C. In F–Se@CDs, first weight loss (33%) took place at 420 °C and second weight loss (27%) at 990 °C. In S–Se@CDs first weight loss (32%) observed at 390 °C and second weight loss (38%) observed at 990 °C. From the obtained data, it can be inferred that first weight loss majorly took place due to the evaporation of water molecules and second weight loss arises due to the decomposition of functional groups (carboxylic ad hydroxyl) present on the surface of Se@CDs. In developed Se@CDs nanohybrids, majorly 70% of weight loss takes place which shows that 30% of nanohybrid composite does not break even at very high temperature. Further, DSC analysis has been carried out to explore the heating behavior and type of reaction involved in prepared Se@CDs nanohybrids (Fig. 3(d-f)). From the obtained DSC spectrum, it was observed that, the developed Se@CDs system exhibits exothermic type of reaction which suggests that on heating the system, it has the tendency to release heat. From here it can be concluded that the prepared Se@CDs exhibits good thermal behavior.

The HRTEM analysis has been explored out to evaluate the topographical alteration and size range profile of developed Se@CDs (Fig. 4 (a-c)) nanohybrid system. It was observed that all the three developed system (S–Se@CDs, F–Se@CDs and G-Se@CDs) reflects uniformly dispersed homogenous spherical morphology. The average size range of developed Se@CDs was found to be in range of 4–7 nm. The SAED pattern of developed system has also been provided (Fig. 4(a–c) inset). The representation of lattice fringes and more insight view of developed Se@CDs have been represented in Fig. S5. which provides better information about the system. Further to investigate the morphological alteration, FESEM analysis has been carried (Fig. 4(d-f)). During SEM analysis, the surface of Se@CDs scanned through focused beam of an electron which interacts with the atoms present on the surface during which charges gathered to form the image. The developed system suggested the excellent dispensability with uniform dispersion of spherical morphology particles. The FESEM results have been found totally corresponded to the obtained HRTEM results. The regular size of developed Se@CDs was found to be 40–50 nm. From the obtained images it was found very much clear the Se supported CDs has been developed completely due to the formation sharp edges on the surface. To evaluate the purity of developed Se@CDs, EDX analysis has been carried out. From the EDX spectrum, it was observed that Se, carbon and oxygen element were present as major constituents and nitrogen as minor constituent in S–Se@CDs, F–Se@CDs and G-Se@CDs. The obtained data suggest the ultimate purity of prepared Se@CDs with the absence of undesired peak. Further the mapping analysis of obtained elements have been carried out which have been represented in Fig. S6. The mapping analysis also confirmed the presence of C, O, N and Se elements in their periphery. From here, it can be concluded that the developed Se@CDs nano hybrid systems have only preferred elements which suggest the ultimate purity of the system [28–30]. Further, to investigate the charge present on the surface of developed Se@CDs, zeta-potential analysis was carried out Fig. S7. Here, water was used as dispersant with refractive index value of 1.33, viscosity = 088, dielectric constant = 78.5, temperature = 25 °C and clear disposable zeta cell was used. The zeta potential values of S–Se@CDs, F–Se@CDs and G-Se@CDs were found to be −1.16, −4.36 and −18.7 (mV) whereas value of conductivity was reported 1.03, 0.69 and 4.02 (mS/cm). From here, it can be concluded that developed Se@CDs nano hybrid contains specific amount of negative charge on their surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of developed Se@CDs in tetracycline sensing
ce:section.ce:para: Tetracycline (TC) is considered as the most widely adopted antibiotic around the world. But its excessive utilization is creating a series of problems amongst the environmental species. So, it is mandatory to optimize and detect the amount of TC in different water resources. While keeping all the facts in mind, the prepared F–Se@CDs, G-Se@CDs and S–Se@CDs nano hybrids were utilized for the effective sensing of TC. Before carrying out sensing study, the excitation dependent behaviour of developed Se@CDs nano hybrid system was evaluated out in range 280–330 nm which shows that maximum excitation and emission found at 290 and 430 nm (Fig. S8). Further the emission spectrum of the developed F–Se@CDs G-Se@CDs and S–Se@CDs nano hybrids was investigated in presence of tetracycline (TC) and it was observed that 12, 40 and 14% (Fig. S9) of quenching takes place. The obtained results show that maximum quenching (40%) of TC took place in case of G-Se@CDs. In case of fructose five membered ring is present, whereas in glucose six membered ring is present. Fructose is considered as more strained structure for the delocalization of electrons and charge transfer as compared to the glucose structure. In case of sucrose, both five and six membered combined rings are present which is considered as the stable structure and does not have enough surface area for the delocalization. From here, it can be considered that glucose reduced Se@CDs shows effective quenching because of complete charge transfer. Due to the respective reason, only G-Se@CDs has been further utilized in the sensing behavior of TC.

Tetracycline can be efficiently utilized for the quenching of emission profile of G-Se@CDs through fluorescence spectroscopy. To evaluate the sensitivity and influential behavior of TC amount, the concentration dependent emission behavior of TC was studied in range 1000–10 μM (Fig. 5 (a)). From the emission spectrum, it was observed that on decreasing the concentration from 1000 to 10 μM, the emission profile decreases linearly. From here, it can be evaluated out that on decreasing the concentration of TC in G-Se@CDs nanohybrid system, the emission intensity diminishes in great manner. Additionally, bathochromic (red) shift was observed in G-Se@CDs emission peak upon the addition of different concentration of TC which suggest that there is strong complex formation took place between G-Se@CDs and TC due to electrostatic interactions. Further, it is very important to explore the sensitivity and selectivity of respected system in presence of different interfering analytes (cysteine, arginine, serine, tryptophan, histidine, glutamic acid (GA), aspartic acid (AA), Ba2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Na+, Pb2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, oxytetracycline (OxTc), and doxycycline (Dxc)). The fixed concentration (10 μM) of all the interfering analytes were utilized to investigate the linear effect of quenching (Fig. 5(b)). The fluorescence spectrum of the prepared samples was observed in range 200–800 nm. Before observing the emission profile, all the samples were mixed well and sonicated for 10 min. After sonication process, all the samples were kept undisturbed at room temperature for 5 min. Then, their emission profiles have been evaluated out. From the emission spectrum, it was observed that, no significant amount of variation was observed in TC profile after the addition of interfering analytes. From here, it can be inferred that the respective system (G-Se@CDs) shows the ultimate selectivity towards TC in presence of various other analytes [31–33].

Further, the interference data have been utilized to plot relative intensity plot and normalized intensity plot to investigate the efficacy of the system (Fig. S10). As pH play very sensitive role in biological chemical systems, it is important to evaluate the pH study of developed system. The pH study of TC in presence of G-Se@CDs was demonstrated to evaluate their stability in acidic and basic medium (Fig. 5(c)). From the obtained emission spectrum, it was inferred that on increasing the pH of system from 2 to 10, the emission intensity augmented linearly. But after pH = 10, there is a slight decrement in the emission intensity was observed. It is because on further increasing the pH of the system, the concentration of hydroxide ions increases which further acts as a templating agent over the surface of G-Se@CDs due to which TC does not get effective site for the complexation. From here, it may be inferred that the developed sensor works efficiently at pH = 10.

The mechanistic quenching behavior of fluorescent probe in presence of analyte is related to the inner filter effect and charge transfer mechanism. During inner filter effect (IFE), efficient overlapping and shift takes place between emission band of fluorescent probe (Se@CDs) and analyte (TC). The IFE basically deals with the spectral distortion. The analyte can effectively absorb the emission light of fluorescent probe and led to effective fundamental quenching. From the literature, it was observed that TC shows absorption band at 350 nm whereas developed G-Se@CDs shows emission in 280–550 nm. From here it can be concluded that TC can efficiently absorb excitation and emission light of Se@CDs [34,35]. Secondly, different functional groups (carboxylic and amine groups) are present on the surface of developed G-Se@CDs which has very high affinity to bind with the ketonic and hydroxyl group present on the surface of TC i.e., strong chemical interaction occurs between them which leads to quenching of emission intensity of G-Se@CDs by TC (Scheme 1 ). The stern-Volmer plot (F/Fo vs conc.) was utilized (standard 3σ IUPAC criteria) to evaluate the value of detection limit (3S.D/slope where S.D corresponds to the standard deviation of system) and quantitation limit (10S.D/slope) from the linear fit equation (Fo/F = 1+Ksv[conc.]) and was found to be 0.155 μM (155 nM) and 0.517 μM−1 (517 nM−1) with R2 = 0.99. Further, Benesi-Hilderbrand (B–H) plot (1/F-Fo vs 1/conc.) was considered to calculate the value of binding constant (Fig. 6 ) from the following equation:
1
F o
− F
=
1
k
(
F max
−
F o
)
[
conc .
]
+
1
F
max .
−
F o

Where Fo and F corresponds to the intensity of pure Se supported CDs without and with presence of metal ions. The value of binding constant was demonstrated out to be 0.0074 nm−1 with R2 = 0.9.8. All the experimentation has been carried out thrice to evaluate the more accurate data. The obtained results suggest that developed G-Se@CDs system shows good amount of value which is very much higher than the permissible limit. Till date, various methods have been published for the sensing of TC but they have various drawbacks such as high instrumentation cost, time consuming, labor cost and etc. But the proposed system utilized simple preparation method, less time consumption and high quantum yield values. The comparison of the present work with the reported literature on sensing of tetracycline has been represented in Table S5. The developed system works as a highly selective and intriguing platform towards TC sensing [36–38].

The practical application of developed G-Se@CDs in presence of tetracycline have been evaluated out using different types of real sample (lake water, well water, rain water, tap water and river water) to explore the reliability and accuracy of the system (Fig. 7 (a)). Additionally, the recovery of TC was also evaluated out in raw milk, honey, packed juices, mixture of sugars (glucose, fructose and sucrose) and paracetamol (PCM) (Fig. 7(b)). The standard deviation method was explored to evaluate the efficacy of system. Different concentration of TC (50, 100, 200, 300 and 400 μM) was utilized in presence of G-Se@CDs to evaluate the efficacy of the system. Fluorescence (emission) spectroscopic analysis have been carried out for the prepared samples. The change in the emission intensity was calculated out by applying simple linear calibration method. It was observed that the developed system shows 80–90% recovery in presence of different mediums which suggest the ultimate specific potential of the system. The developed G-Se@CDs hybrid system works well in all the present medium. All the experimentation has been carried out three times to evaluate the reproducibility of the system and error percentage. From the obtained results, it can be clearly demonstrated that the developed G-Se@CDs nanohybrid system reflects ultimate selectivity towards TC and can be utilized as a sensor for the promising application in actual speedy manner [7,39].

In summary, development of Se@CDs nano hybrid system was carried out using simple reducing sugars (fructose, glucose and sucrose) after applying simple, rapid and one step methodology. The different analytical studies were investigated on developed nano hybrid to evaluate their physical and chemical properties. The developed system exhibits excellent absorption and emission properties. The outstanding fluorescence behaviour and excellent physicochemical properties of developed G-Se@CDs (glucose reduced Se@CDs) were further utilized in the field of fluorometric antibiotic sensing especially tetracycline. The developed sensor was found to be highly sensitive and selective towards TC in presence of different interfering ions. The value of detection limit and binding constant of Se@CDs against TC was found to be 155 nM and 0.0074 nM−1. Further, the specificity of developed sensors was evaluated out in different real water sample and other sources present in the environment which suggest their excellent recovery.

Pooja Chauhan: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Savita Chaudhary: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Resources, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Pooja Chauhan is thankful to the CSIR open SRF funding 2019. Savita Chaudhary is thankful to DST Inspire Faculty award [IFACH- 17], Haryana State Council for Science and Technology [HSCSIT/R&D/2020/476], DST Chandigarh and DST Purse grants II for financial assistance. Authors are thankful to Panjab University SAIF CIL and Department of Environment Studies Panjab University Chandigarh for technical support.

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.optmat.2021.111638.
